coredata.dc:title: Combining carbon dots and Ag6Si2O7 nanoparticles with TiO2: Visible-light-driven photocatalysts with efficient performance for removal of pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Feizpoor, Solmaz
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Seifzadeh, Davod
Ghosh, Srabanti
coredata.dc:description: Fabrication of TiO2-based materials with high photocatalytic ability can be considered as a significant research topic in the field of heterogeneous photocatalysis. Herein, ternary photocatalysts composed of TiO2, Ag6Si2O7, and C-Dots were fabricated through a simple procedure and various techniques were applied to evaluate the photoelectrochemical, optical, textural, structural, and morphological features of the as-obtained photocatalysts. The TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots nanocomposite with 0.75 mL of C-Dots indicated greater photoactivity in degradation of fuchsine, rhodamine B, methyl orange, and methylene blue, which was almost 18.1, 24.4, 13.8, and 24.4-times as high as the pristine TiO2 and 3.1, 4.7, 2.8, and 3.6-folds as high as the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) nanocomposite, respectively. It was found that formation of n-n heterojunctions among Ag6Si2O7 and TiO2 counterparts and properties of C-Dots lead to prolonged charge lifetime and increased visible-light absorption, leading to impressively improved photocatalytic performance. The quenching tests proved that h+ and •O2 − species possess substantial influence on the degradation reactions. In light of the experimental results, the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots nanocomposites can be regarded as highly effective photocatalysts to eliminate toxic contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water pollutants
TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots
Visible-light photocatalyst
Photocatalytic performance

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD profiles of the obtained photocatalysts.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst.
EDX spectra of the as-synthesized photocatalysts.
XPS data for the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample: (a) Survay spectrum, and the spectra for: (b) Ti 2p, (c) Ag 3d, (d) Si 2p, (e) O 1 s, and (f) C 1 s.
(a) UV–Vis DR spectra and (b) FT-IR of the as-prepared samples.

(a) Photodagradation of RhB by various prepared photocatalysts. Absorption spectra of RhB at different time intervals under visible light on the (b) TiO2, (c) TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and (d) TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts.
(a) The rate constants for photodegradation of RhB on the prepared samples and (b) N2 sorption data for the TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts.
(a) Transient photocurrent responses, (b) PL, and (c) EIS of the prepared photocatalysts.
Rate constants for photodegradation of RhB in presence of different scavengers on the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst.
Mott–Schottky plots for the (a) TiO2 and (b) Ag6Si2O7 samples. (c) Mechanism of photocatalytic reaction processes on the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots heterojunction photocatalysts.
Recycling runs toward degradation of RhB over the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite.
The degradation rate constants of different pollutants on the prepared photocatalysts.
Schematic presentation for synthesis of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots nanocomposites.
Textural properties of the TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Combining carbon dots and Ag6Si2O7 nanoparticles with TiO2: Visible-light-driven photocatalysts with efficient performance for removal of pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Inorganic Chemistry, Institute of Chemical Research “Andrés M. del Río” (IQAR), Universidad de Alcalá, Campus Universitario, 28805-Alcalá de Henares, Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic and Inorganic Chemistry
Institute of Chemical Research “Andrés M. del Río” (IQAR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Inorganic Chemistry, Institute of Chemical Research “Andrés M. del Río” (IQAR), Universidad de Alcalá, Campus Universitario, 28805-Alcalá de Henares, Madrid, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of TiO2-based materials with high photocatalytic ability can be considered as a significant research topic in the field of heterogeneous photocatalysis. Herein, ternary photocatalysts composed of TiO2, Ag6Si2O7, and C-Dots were fabricated through a simple procedure and various techniques were applied to evaluate the photoelectrochemical, optical, textural, structural, and morphological features of the as-obtained photocatalysts. The TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots nanocomposite with 0.75 mL of C-Dots indicated greater photoactivity in degradation of fuchsine, rhodamine B, methyl orange, and methylene blue, which was almost 18.1, 24.4, 13.8, and 24.4-times as high as the pristine TiO2 and 3.1, 4.7, 2.8, and 3.6-folds as high as the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) nanocomposite, respectively. It was found that formation of n-n heterojunctions among Ag6Si2O7 and TiO2 counterparts and properties of C-Dots lead to prolonged charge lifetime and increased visible-light absorption, leading to impressively improved photocatalytic performance. The quenching tests proved that h+ and •O2 − species possess substantial influence on the degradation reactions. In light of the experimental results, the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots nanocomposites can be regarded as highly effective photocatalysts to eliminate toxic contaminants.

ce:section.ce:para: In the 21st century, environmental pollution is an arising worry in the light of fast industrialization, rapid population growth, and changing lifestyle. Consequently, water pollution has become an urgent problem that the governments have faced at the present time. Accordingly, it is necessary to eliminate the pollutants from the surface waters owing to the degradation-resistant nature, high toxicity, and bioaccumulation potential [1–4]. Hitherto, plenty of technologies and methods have been introduced to eradicate organic contaminants from the surface waters such as chemical reactions, electrochemical conversion, flotation, adsorption, biological removal, ion exchange, sedimentation, ozone oxidation, Fenton reaction, filtration, and etc. However, these common techniques possess several disadvantages, like high energy and time-consumption, high operative costs, and inability to completely remove the contaminants, which seriously prevented their extensive application [5–7]. Therefore, it is required to discover an efficient strategy to overcome the limitations of traditional techniques. Presently, semiconductor-based photocatalysis has attracted much more attention because of its no secondary pollution, high degradation efficiency at normal temperature and pressure, simplicity of the process, and affordability [8–23]. Among different semiconductors, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been introduced as a most fascinating photocatalyst because it has significant non-toxicity, low cost, chemical stability, and abundance. However, rapid recombination of the charge carriers and large band gap of TiO2 (3.2 eV) considerably diminish the photocatalytic efficiency and limit the utilization of solar energy, which restrict its wide application in photocatalytic processes [24–30]. Hence, enhancing the visible-light absorption characteristics and decreasing the recombination of charge carriers are two main issues in the fabrication of TiO2-based photocatalysts.

Ag-based semiconductors like Ag3VO4, Ag2CrO4, AgBr, Ag2O, Ag3PO4, and Ag2CO3, have recently drawn much consideration in the photocatalytic processes because of their high photoresponse in visible range [31–33]. Among various Ag-based semiconductors, silver silicate (Ag6Si2O7) possesses suitable band gap (∼1.6 eV), which can allow excellent mobility for the carrier charges and good absorption of visible light [34–36]. Moreover, Ag6Si2O7 is an n-type semiconductor. Consequently, to enhance the photocatalytic activity of n-type TiO2, constructing an n-n heterojunction system between these semiconductors sounds an appropriate way to boost the transportation of photoinduced charges. Besides, owing to the up-conversion feature of carbon dots (C-Dots), it broads the light usage range through absorbing lights with large wavelengths and emitting low wavelengths [37,38]. Although many efforts have been made to fabricate TiO2-based photocatalysts, to the best of our knowledge, fabrication of TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots photocatalyst and study of their photocatalytic ability have not been reported.

In this work, we successfully fabricated TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots nanocomposites and XRD, UV–vis DRS, EDX, XPS, TEM, HRTEM, EIS, BET, FT-IR, photocurrent density, and PL instruments were used to analyze the phase, purity, optical, chemical, morphological, textural, and electrochemical properties of the resultant photocatalysts. The TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst exhibited the greatest photocatalytic performance in degradation of fuchsine, RhB, and MB as cationic dyes, and MO as an anionic dye, which was almost 18.1, 24.4, 24.4, and 13.8-times as high as the TiO2 and 3.1, 4.7, 3.6, and 2.8-folds as high as the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) nanocoposite, respectively. It was found that formation of heterojunctions among TiO2, C-Dots, and Ag6Si2O7 counterparts lead to increased visible-light absorption and improved charge carrier separation, resulting in impressively improved photocatalytic performance. Finally, the durability of the optimum photocatalyst was studied via successive recycling experiments.

By using a TOPCON (EM-002B) microscope, the morphology of the materials was presented. A Cecile 9000 UV–vis spectrophotometer was applied to investigate the optical properties of the samples. The BET analysis was conducted on a Belsorp Mini II instrument. A JEOL (JPS-9010MC) photoelectron spectrometer was employed to evaluate the XPS spectra. A Bandelin ultrasound generator of HD 3100 was utilized for the ultrasonic treatment of the samples. A Perkin Elmer, RX I instrument was utilized to provide FT-IR spectra. The XRD patterns were analyzed on a diffractometer (Philips Xpert) with Cu Kα radiation. The PL technique was measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS 55, Perkin Elmer). A µAutolabIII Potentiostat/Galvanostat equipped with three electrodes was utilized to study the photoelectrochemical properties. More information has been provided in our recent work [39].

The TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) photocatalyst was synthesized by sonicating 0.4 g of TiO2 in 100 mL of water for 5 min. In the next step, silver nitratesolution (0.271 g, 25 mL) and sodium metasilicate nonahydrate solution (0.067 g, 25 mL) were sequentially appended to the suspension. After 2 h stirring, the solution was refluxed for one hour. Subsequently, the precipitate was washed with water and ethanol and kept in an oven for drying at 50 °C for 16 h. The TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots samples with various quantities of C-Dots (0.25, 0. 5, 0.75, and 1 mL) were synthesized by a simple procedure. The C-Dots was obtained according to the reported procedure [40]. For example, 1 mL of C-Dots aqueous solution (5 mg/mL) and 0.4 g of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) nanocomposite were sonicated for 10 min. In the end, the obtained sample was dried overnight at 60 °C (Scheme 1 ).

The photocatalytic abilities of the photocatalysts (100 mg) were investigated by degradation of 250 mL aqueous solutions of RhB, MO, and MB (1.0 × 10-5 mol/L), and fuchsine (0.77 × 10-5 mol/L). Equilibrium of adsorption/desorption was achieved by stirring the mixtures in dark condition for 1 h. The visible-light source (LED lamp of 50 W) was mounted on the top of the reactor about 20 cm from the liquid surface. Prior to the irradiation, at every 30 min, about 3 mL of the suspensions were taken out, filtered for separation of the photocatalysts, and studied spectrophotometrically at 553, 477, 664, and 540 nm, respectively to detect their concentrations.

To examine the crystal structure of the nanocomposites, XRD analysis was carried out. Fig. 1 represents the XRD patterns of TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples. The observed diffraction peaks display that TiO2 has a tetragonal structure, which is identical to the JCPDS standard of 04–0477 [41]. For the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) sample, diffraction peaks are assigned to the tetragonal phase of TiO2 and monoclinic phase of Ag6Si2O7 [36]. In the XRD diffraction peaks of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample, no obvious peaks related to C-Dots nanoparticles were detected because of its low content [42].

The TEM and HRTEM analyses were conducted to study the morphological features of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite. The TEM picture in Fig. 2 a clearly shows the spherical-like morphology of TiO2, in which small particles of Ag6Si2O7 and C-Dots anchored on its surface. To observe the detailed structure of the ternary nanocomposite, the HRTERM analysis was employed. According to Fig. 2b, the interplanar spacing of 0.320 and 0.277 nm are ascribed to the C-Dots (0 0 2) and Ag6Si2O7 (1 1 0) planes, while 0.352 nm is equivalent to the (1 0 1) lattice of TiO2 [36,43].

The elemental composition of the samples was further investigated by EDX instrument. As seen in Fig. 2 , the TiO2 photocatalyst composed of O and Ti elements. Moreover, O, Ti, C, Si, and Ag elements were observed in the EDX profile of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite. The results of the EDX analysis proved that the as-synthesized photocatalysts are very pure, which are in line with the XRD outcomes.

To study the bonding modality of elements and components on the surface of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite, XPS was applied. The XPS survey scan of the sample expresses the presence of Ti, Si, Ag, C, and O elements, and it is illustrated in Fig. 4 (a). For the Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 4b), the peaks can be ascribed to the 458.4 (Ti 2p3/2) and 464.3 eV (Ti 2p1/2), indicating that Ti is in + 4 oxidation state [44]. Fig. 4c displays that Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 peaks of Ag6Si2O7 are at 374.5 and 368.5 eV, respectively [45]. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the binding energies of Si 2p3/2 and Si 2p1/2 are 101.8 and 102.7 eV, respectively, which suggests that Si element exists as Si4+ [45]. The O1s spectrum is seen in Fig. 4e. The peaks at 531.51, 530.04, and 529.4 eV are well match with the Si-O, Ag-O, and Ti-O bonds, respectively [46]. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4f) consists of two peaks at 288.8 and 285.01 eV, which are compatible with the C = O and C-C groups, respectively [39].

Heterogeneous photocatalytic processes are started by absorption of light with energy higher than the bandgap of photocatalyst to produce electron-hole pairs. Hence, visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity is strictly depending on visible-light harvesting of photocatalysts. Accordingly, the ability of the samples to visible-light absorption was studied by UV–vis DR spectra. As outlined in Fig. 5 a, the bare TiO2 shows a clear absorption edge around 390 nm without any absorbance in the visible region, because of its wide band gap of 3.2 eV. The TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) sample demonstrates the enhancement of light-harvesting capability compared to the TiO2. After incorporating C-Dots with the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) sample, the light absorption capacity of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots nanocomposites has been significantly increased. It can be seen that for the ternary nanocomposites, the absorption intensity in the visible-light region increases with increasing value of C-Dots, which is benefit for producing more electron–hole pairs under visible light. The results suggest that the photoresponse of TiO2 in the visible region can be considerably enhanced after combining C-Dots and Ag6Si2O7, which leads to produce more electron-hole pairs under visible light and promote photocatalytic efficiency in this region. FT-IR spectroscopy was implemented to evaluate the chemical bonds of the TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts (Fig. 5b). The broad band at 500 to 800 cm−1 is developed owing to the Ti-O-Ti vibration [47]. The characteristic peak related to the O-H bond is located at 3300–3500 cm−1 [48]. For the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples, the peaks related to the Si-OH and Si-O bonds are located at 1090 and 810 cm−1, respectively [36]. The spectrum of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample displays the existence of peaks at 1610 and 1415 cm−1 belonging to C = O and C = C bonds, respectively, which is ascribed to the C-Dots [43].

To assess the photocatalytic properties of the samples, the photodegradation of RhB under visible light was done, and the related outcomes are indicated in Fig. 6 a. The photocatalytic removal of RhB can be ignored without using the photocatalyst. The concentration of RhB didn’t show outstanding changes over the TiO2 under visible light. Indeed, the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) sample displays RhB degradation of about 49.1% in 120 min. Compared with the binary photocatalyst and bare TiO2, the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots samples have remarkably boosted. As seen, the photocatalytic ability of TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots samples is affected by the amount of C-Dots nanoparticles. The RhB degradation rate boosts with C-Dots content up to 0.75 mL and dwindles for the nanocomposite containing 1 mL. The degradation efficiency of RhB for the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst is nearly 99.8% after 120 min, manifestly the highest performance in comparison with the other samples. The intensity variations of RhB absorption spectra in the presence of the TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts under various reaction times are displayed in Fig. 6 (b-d). The intensity of RhB absorption peak reduces with the lengthening of the light irradiation for 2 h and nearly 12.6, 49.1, and 99.8% of RhB were eliminated using the TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) powders.

Fig. 7 presents that the removal of RhB conforms the first-order model. The apparent removal rate constants are 334 × 10-4, 71.1 × 10-4, and 13.7 × 10-4 min−1 for the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL), TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2 samples, respectively. According to the outcomes, the highest removal rate constant is related to the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample, which is 24.4 and 4.7 times faster than the removal rate constants of TiO2 and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) samples, respectively. Therefore, incorporating Ag6Si2O7 and C-Dots nanoparticles with TiO2 can be a beneficial strategy to achieve high photocatalytic efficiency. To evaluate the porosity of the samples, as one of the effective factors affecting photocatalytic ability, BET analyses were conducted and the results are presented in Fig. 7b. The BET analysis proved that the isotherms for TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples classified as the type-IV nitrogen isotherm, specifying the existence of mesopores. The BET surface area of 45.3, 46.5, and 49.2 m2/g was obtained for the TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples, respectively. The BET surface area of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite is higher than the other samples. The increased surface area of the ternary sample led to enhance active sites for the photodegradation process. Table 1 indicates more details about the textural properties of the samples.

The PL, photocurrent, and EIS tests are effective measurements for evaluating the recombination/separation rate of charge carriers, which play crucial roles in the increment of the photocatalytic activity. Fig. 8 a depicts the current intensity of TiO2, TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%), and TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples. As seen, the ternary sample possesses the highest photocurrent response, displaying that effective charge separation can be accomplished in this sample. Fig. 8b exhibits the PL spectra of the samples by an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The lower PL intensity expresses the higher migration of carriers during photocatalytic process, leading to higher photocatalytic efficiency. As illustrated in Fig. 8b, the PL intensity of the photocatalysts can be listed as follows: TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) < TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) < TiO2. It is observed that the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite possesses the weakest PL intensity, which is in good consistent with its high photoactivity. To further corroborate the high migration ability of the photo-induced electron and hole, EIS analysis was accomplished. It is worth noting that the arc radius of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite is smaller than the other samples. Based on the outcomes, synergistic interactions between TiO2, Ag6Si2O7, and C-Dots may cause a reduced rate for recombination of the photo-induced charges, which cause the enhancement of photocatalytic ability.

To explain the role of the reactive species in the photodegradation processes, radical scavenging tests were conducted with the existence of various radical scavengers and the related results are indicated in Fig. 9 . The main reactive radicals, such as h+, •O2 −, and •OH, were suppressed using the introduction of ammonium oxalate (AO), benzoquinone (BQ), and 2-propanol (2-PrOH), respectively. The removal rate of RhB over the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample was reduced markedly in the presence of BQ and AO, while the degradation rate was affected slightly in the presence of 2-PrOH. Therefore, the results defined that h+ and •O2 − play a key role in the photodegradation reaction.

Mott–Schottky (M−S) curve is an impressive method to determine the flat band position (Vfb) and type of semiconductors. As seen in Fig. 10 (a, b), the positive slope of M−S curves prove that TiO2 and Ag6Si2O7 are n-type samples. The flat-band potentials of TiO2 and Ag6Si2O7 are detected to be −0.6 and + 0.34 V vs. Ag/AgCl, corresponding to −0.4 and 0.54 V (vs. NHE) [49]. The difference between the flat-band potential of an n-type semiconductor and the conduction band potential is about 0.1 eV [50]. Thus, the corresponding conduction band of TiO2 and Ag6Si2O7 are determined to be −0.50 and + 0.44 eV. Also, the valance band of TiO2 and Ag6Si2O7 are calculated to be −2.7 and 2.02 eV using the empirical formula of ECB = EVB – Eg. Based on the results, a possible mechanism for RhB degradation on the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots nanocomposites has been suggested in Fig. 10c. As displayed, Ag6Si2O7 can form n-n heterojunction with TiO2. The Fermi levels (EF) of n-type TiO2 and Ag6Si2O7 are close to their ECB [32,36]. When TiO2 and Ag6Si2O7 form n-n heterojunctions, electrons transfer from TiO2 to Ag6Si2O7, due to the more negative EF of TiO2.The EF of TiO2 is shifted downward whereas the EF of Ag6Si2O7 is shifted upward until their Fermi levels are aligned. When the ternary TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots photocatalyst is irradiated by visible light, electrons and holes are generated on Ag6Si2O7. Moreover, C-Dots with upconversion feature converts lower energy lights to higher energy lights for excitation of TiO2 [39]. The electrons presented in the CB of Ag6Si2O7 move to the CB of TiO2. Therefore, the electrons on TiO2 and Ag6Si2O7 could react with O2 to generate •O2 –. Also, C-Dots acts as an electron acceptor and it can easily trap photogenerated electrons from the CB of Ag6Si2O7 and the trapped electrons on C-Dots react with O2 to produce more •O2 – radicals. Furthermore, h+ in the VB of Ag6Si2O7 can oxidize RhB molecules. Thereby, the photoinduced electrons and holes are separated and led to the boosted photocatalytic activity.

Photostability of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample, as a valuable index for its widespread usage, was assessed by four repeated cycles. As observed in Fig. 11 , no evident reduction of photodegradation efficiency was detected during the removal of RhB in these tests. Hence, it can be deduced that the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample possesses a good potential for utilization in the photodegradation reactions.

To prove the high potential of the photocatalyst in degradation of a large spectrum of contaminants, removal of MB, fuchsine, and MO over the as-obtained photocatalysts was surveyed upon visible-light irradiation (Fig. 12 ). Obviously, the ternary TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst exhibited the superior capacity in the removal of these dyes. According to the degradation rate constants, the photocatalytic ability of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst is 18.1, 13.8, and 24.4-folds as high as the TiO2 for elimination of fuchsine, MO and MB, respectively, while 3.1, 2.8, and 3.6 times greater than that of the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) photocatalyst, respectively. Therefore, the outcomes of photocatalytic tests corroborate that the as-obtained sample has excellent potency in the elimination of various organic contaminants from wastewater.

Briefly, the ternary TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots photocatalysts were synthesized, which displayed premier photocatalytic ability for elimination of various contaminants upon visible-light exposure in comparison to the bare and binary photocatalysts. Kinetic study of the photodegradation reactions presented that the removal rate constants of fuchsine, RhB, MO, and MB on the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst were nearly 18.1, 24.4, 13.8, and 24.4 times greater than the TiO2, and 3.1, 4.7, 2.8, and 3.6 folds as high as the TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 (20%) sample, respectively. The trapping experiments showed that h+ and •O2 − have substantial influence on the degradation reactions. This work could suggest new ideas for fabricating effective heterogeneous photocatalysts for environmental treatment.

Solmaz Feizpoor: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing - original draft. Aziz Habibi-Yangjeh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Davod Seifzadeh: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Srabanti Ghosh: Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of present work by University of Mohaghegh Ardabili-Iran is acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials-enabled water and wastewater treatment
NanoImpact

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, B. Wu, H. Xu, H. Liu, M. Wang, Y. He, B. Pan, Nanomaterials-enabled water and wastewater treatment, NanoImpact 3 (2016) 22–39.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A critical review on textile wastewater treatments: Possible approaches
J. Environ. Manage.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.R. Holkar, A.J. Jadhav, D.V. Pinjari, N.M. Mahamuni, A.B. Pandit, A critical review on textile wastewater treatments: Possible approaches, Journal of Environmental Management 182 (2016) 351–366.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in floating TiO2-based photocatalysts for environmental application
Appl. Catal. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xing, J. Zhang, J. Cui, J. Yin, T. Zhao, J. Kuang, Z. Xiu, N. Wan, W. Zhou, Recent advances in floating TiO2-based photocatalysts for environmental application, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental 225 (2017) 452–467.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Light-assisted advanced oxidation processes for the elimination of chemical and microbiological pollution of wastewaters in developed and developing Countries
Molecules
